nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef ecosystems; reef slopes, lagoons, and back reef areas rich in hard and soft coral growth, typically at depths of 3–20 m

diet.notes: In the wild tends to feed on coral polyps, soft corals, small invertebrates, and zooplankton. In captivity, has been reported to initially resist prepared foods; offering live fortified brine shrimp, frozen mysis, chopped clams, or blackworms is generally advised to encourage feeding. Once acclimated, most individuals have been known to accept a variety of frozen and prepared meaty foods, supplemented with herbivore preparations.

avoidWith: other butterflyfish unless a bonded pair

summary: A striking Red Sea endemic with bright golden-yellow coloration, bold orange stripes, and a distinctive blue-gray cheek patch. Generally considered peaceful toward other fish but not reef safe, as it has been documented feeding on coral polyps and small invertebrates.
careNotes:
Chaetodon semilarvatus is endemic to the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, where it tends to inhabit coral-rich reef slopes and back reef lagoons at depths of roughly 3–20 m. It is generally considered one of the more hardy butterflyfishes once established, but new arrivals can be initially reluctant to accept prepared foods — offering live or fresh meaty foods (clams, fortified brine shrimp, blackworms) is commonly advised before transitioning to frozen and flake fare.

This species is not reef safe; it has been documented feeding on soft and LPS corals, tubeworms, and has been known to nip at Tridacna clams. It is best kept in a fish-only (FO) or fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) system with ample open swimming space and rock work for shelter.

In the wild it tends to form monogamous pairs that remain bonded for life. In aquariums it is generally possible to keep a small group provided all individuals are introduced simultaneously and the tank is large enough (600 L or more for a group). Best kept singly or as a mated pair in typical home aquarium setups.

Source cross-verification notes: Adult maximum size reported as 22.9–23 cm by LiveAquaria, FishBase, MarineBio, and AquaticCommunity; ReefApp reported 28 cm. Mainstream consensus is ~23 cm max; 28 cm may reflect an exceptional specimen or measurement methodology difference. Temperature ranges differed somewhat: LiveAquaria and AquaticCommunity gave 22–25.6°C while FishBase wild data showed 24.7–29.3°C; averaged range used (23–27°C). AquaticCommunity listed unusually high salinity (1.028–1.035); this outlier was excluded in favor of the mainstream range (1.020–1.026) consistent with LiveAquaria, ReefApp, and general Red Sea husbandry guidance. Tank size recommendations ranged from 300 L (AquaticCommunity) to 600 L (ReefApp); averaged value ~450 L used.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to form monogamous pairs that may spawn in large, well-established aquariums, but successful captive breeding to the point of raising fry has rarely been documented. Like most butterflyfishes, larvae are pelagic and extremely difficult to rear in captivity. Generally considered 'not in captivity' for practical breeding purposes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used in place of the Reef2Reef thread (which returned HTTP 403). Provided max size 23 cm TL, temperature range 24.7–29.3°C, native range, taxonomy, and habitat data. Confirmed species identification as Chaetodon semilarvatus (Cuvier, 1831), family Chaetodontidae.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (28 cm — higher than other sources), tank size (600 L), temperature (~26°C), salinity (SG 1.026), diet, and reef-safe status.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (23 cm), temperature (72–78°F / 22–25.5°C), pH (8.1–8.4), tank size (80 gal / 300 L), diet, and reef-safe status. Salinity listed as 1.028–1.035 which is inconsistent with all other sources and was treated as an outlier.

additional.name: MarineBio
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 23 cm, native range (Red Sea and Gulf of Aden), habitat (coral reef lagoons and reef slopes), diet, monogamous pair behavior, and lifespan (up to ~10 years for similar species).

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic larvae are extremely difficult to raise; captive breeding to successful fry stage has rarely if ever been achieved. Forms monogamous pairs in the wild.
